definitions.definition: A function is a relation between a set of inputs (called the domain) and a set of permissible outputs (called the range). It associates each input with exactly one output.
The set of all possible input values (x-values) for which the function is defined.
The set of all possible output values (y-values) that the function can produce.
The set of all possible output values (y-values) for which the function is defined.
A function is said to be one-to-one if each element in the range is the image of at most one element from the domain.
A function is said to be onto if every element in the codomain is the image of at least one element from the domain.
A function is said to be bijective if it is both one-to-one and onto.
A relation is a set of ordered pairs.
A relation is said to be an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
A relation R on a set A is said to be reflexive if (a, a) is an element of R for every element a in A.
A relation R on a set A is said to be symmetric if (a, b) is an element of R and (b, a) is also an element of R for every element a, b in A.
A relation R on a set A is said to be transitive if (a, b) is an element of R and (b, c) is an element of R, then (a, c) is also an element of R for every element a, b, c in A.
The composition of two functions f and g is denoted as (f o g)(x) = f(g(x)).
The identity function is a function that maps every element of the domain to itself.
The inverse of a function f is a function denoted as f^(-1) that maps the output of the original function back to the input.
